name: acacia confusa
intensity: High (with MAOI); mild otherwise
mechanism: Primary psychoactive activity is attributed to N, N-DMT agonism at serotonin receptors (especially 5-HT2A); oral activity generally requires MAO inhibition to reduce first-pass metabolism.
interactions: Stimulants that raise blood pressure/heart rate
safetyNotes: Human clinical data are limited for Acacia confusa preparations. Risk increases substantially when combined with MAO inhibitors due to stronger and longer serotonergic effects; avoid use with serotonergic drugs because of serotonin-toxicity risk.

name: Acacia confusa Merr.
intensity: High (especially when used with MAOIs)
mechanism: dmt acts as a 5-ht2a agonist | psychoactive effects enhanced by maois
contraindications: avoid in individuals with psychiatric disorders
safetyNotes: High risk of psychological distress, unsafe in uncontrolled use, Nausea, vomiting, dissociation, confusion, anxiety, Not well-studied, primary risk is psychological or via MAOI combinations, Not established, DMT estimated oral LD50 > 50 mg/kg (in animals).
description: Acacia confusa is a tree native to Southeast Asia used traditionally for spiritual and healing purposes. It contains psychoactive tryptamines like DMT.
dosage: Traditional root bark brew: 5–15g dried bark (not medically recommended)
legalStatus: DMT is scheduled in most countries; plant legality varies
preparation: Brewed as part of analog ayahuasca with MAOI-containing plants.
region: Southeast Asia, Taiwan, Philippines.

name: Acacia maidenii F. Muell.

contraindications: avoid with unstable mental health conditions
interactions: Strong interaction with MAOIs and SSRIs
safetyNotes: Unknown clinical safety, psychological and physiological risks if misused, Confusion, nausea, anxiety, dissociation, Low direct toxicity, high psychological risk, Not established in humans, DMT oral LD50 ~50 mg/kg in rats.

sources.title: NCBI Taxonomy: Acacia maidenii F. Muell.

description: Acacia maidenii is an Australian native tree traditionally used by Aboriginal peoples. It contains tryptamines like DMT and has been studied for its entheogenic potential.
dosage: Not traditionally used in standardized doses; alkaloid extraction for research ranges from 5–15g bark (not recommended for human use)
duration: 4–6 hours (if orally consumed with MAOI; not recommended)
legalStatus: DMT is scheduled; plant legal status varies globally
preparation: Crushed bark sometimes used for DMT extraction (research purposes only).
region: Eastern Australia (NSW, QLD).

name: Myristica fragrans Houtt.
